data.title: Janet Yellen warns Trump's proposed tariffs would reignite inflation
data.description:
Former President Donald Trump's proposed tariffs would reignite inflation and negatively impact the U.S. economy if he were to reclaim the presidency, Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen cautioned in a Thursday speech.

Trump is proposing a blanket 10% tariff on all imported goods entering the U.S. from abroad, alongside imposing tariffs of 60% or higher on imports from China. Such broad-based levies would be inflationary, economists have suggested, on the premise that U.S. consumers would pay for them rather than foreign governments.

Although Yellen did not explicitly mention Trump, she outlined the risks associated with "sweeping, untargeted tariffs," including an increase in "prices for American families," as well as stifling business competition, according to remarks prepared for delivery at a Council of Foreign Relations conference.

"Calls for walling America off with high tariffs on friends and competitors alike, or by treating even our closest allies as transactional partners are deeply misguided," Yellen said.

A Wall Street Journal survey showed a majority of economists think Trump's proposed policies will boost the U.S. government's deficit and increase inflation and interest rates by more than those of Vice President Kamala Harris.

Elsewhere in her remarks, the article said, Yellen underscored how "we need to promote policies, investments and institutions that support global growth, protect financial stability and avoid economic instability."

She touted the U.S.'s "historic economic recovery" from the pandemic era, saying "GDP growth is strong, our unemployment rate is near historic lows, and inflation has declined significantly." Yellen added that productivity growth has been strong, and "more prime-age Americans are participating in our labor force than at any point over the past two decades."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Cboe CEO signals continued interest in acquisitions, if the price is right - report
data.description:
Cboe Global Markets (BATS:CBOE) CEO Fred Tomczyk signalled Thursday that the electronic exchange operator remains open to pursuing acquisitions should the right opportunity present itself.

“We just haven’t found the right target at the right price,” Tomczyk said in an interview with Bloomberg TV. “We don’t want to do an acquisition that doesn’t make a difference.”

Tomczyk stepped into the CEO role just over a year ago following the resignation of former chief Edward Tilly that found he failed to disclose personal relationships with colleagues.

"I'm not in a rush" to name a successor," Tomczyk, 69, told Bloomberg. "But obviously at my age, in my life this is not a long-term thing for me. I just want to make sure it is done in the proper way, and in an orderly way.”

data.title: SolarEdge cut to Sell at Guggenheim on need for 'fundamental reassessment' in Europe
data.description:
SolarEdge Technologies (NASDAQ:SEDG) -4.6% in Thursday's trading as Guggenheim downgrades shares to Sell from Neutral with a $10 price target, while raising Enphase Energy (ENPH) to Neutral from Sell.

While Guggenheim's Joseph Osha believes SolarEdge (SEDG) can maintain its competitive position in commercial markets as a result of buyer focus on quality and security, "the company still appears to have no answer for the continued aggressiveness of Chinese suppliers in European markets for residential inverters."

An adequate response likely will require more than price-cutting, Osha says, seeing the need for a more fundamental reassessment of the company's product lineup in Europe.

In the U.S., the analyst thinks both SolarEdge (SEDG) and Enphase (ENPH) likely will lose share to Tesla's Powerwall 3 in the storage-attached segment of the market, and "investors may be underestimating how quickly the attach rate in the market could rise next year as prices continue to decline and the remaining California NEM 2.0 backlog (which has much lower storage attach rates) is worked off."

With SolarEdge (SEDG) more exposed to the large-developer end of the market as a result of its relationship with Sunrun, Osha believes the competitive impact of Tesla's progress likely will be greater.

The analyst also notes the remaining $350M repayment balance on SolarEdge's (SEDG) 2020 convertible looming next year, which could see overall cash liquidity fall below $300M in next year's Q3, "dangerously low... for an enterprise of SEDG's size" and leaving little margin for error if the business recovers more slowly than management expects.

data.title: Consumer, labor groups urge FTC to block Catalent sale to Novo Nordisk - report
data.description:
Five U.S. consumer groups and two big labor unions are pushing for the Federal Trade Commission to block Novo Nordisk Foundation's planned $16.5 billion acquisition of Catalent (NYSE:CTLT), arguing that a deal would hurt competition in weigh-loss drugs.

U.S. Public Research Group, Service Employees International Union (SEIU) and others expressed their worries about the deal in a letter to the FTC, according a Reuters report on Thursday, which cited the groups. A transaction may curtail options for competitors such as Amgen (OTCPK:AMEN), Pfizer (PFE), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) and AstraZeneca (AZN), according a Reuters report on Thursday, which cited the groups. Other companies developing GLP-1 drugs such as Viking Therapeutics (VKTX), Structure Therapeutics (GPCR) and Sun Pharma may also be affected.

The groups include Consumer Action, Doctors for America and the American Federation of State, County and Municipal Employees (AFSCME) union, according to the report. The organizations are also concerned that Novo's (NVO) ownership could impact Catalent's capacity to make gene therapies.

"The competitive concerns here go far beyond existing drugs," said David Balto, the antitrust lawyer who represents the groups and drafted the letter, according to Reuters. "We believe the commission should look at the impact on future therapies including gene therapy."

Novo Holdings has said it's committed to any existing contracts and isn't aware of any competitive GLP-1 drugs being made at the Catalent (CTLT) sites that it's acquiring.

The letter comes after Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA) a week ago called on the FTC to carefully evaluate the deal.

"I am concerned that this deal could increase Novo Nordisk’s dominance over vital GLP-1 inhibitor drugs, reducing competition and increasing prices for patients," Warren wrote in a letter to FTC Chair Lina Khan last Wednesday.

Catalent (CTLT) and Novo Nordisk Foundation received a request for more information from the FTC in May on their planned deal. In February, Catalent said it had agreed to be acquired by Novo Holdings for $63.50 a share. Novo Holdings will then sell three Catalent manufacturing sites to Novo Nordisk (NVO), which should help increase production of the company’s hugely popular weight-loss drug, Wegovy.

data.title: Equinox Gold sinks on disappointing Ontario mine output, lower guidance
data.description:
Equinox Gold (NYSE:EQX) -6.4% in Thursday's trading after reporting Q3 gold production from its new Greenstone mine in Ontario that missed expectations while lowered guidance for full-year output.

Equinox (EQX) said the mine produced 42.5K oz of gold during Q3 and 59K oz since pouring first gold in May.

Scotiabank analyst Ovais Habib said Equinox (EQX) missed his production estimate of 56.5K oz for the quarter, according to Dow Jones.

During Q3, the mine's processed grade averaged 1.15 g/t at an average recovery rate of ~80%, which the company said was largely on plan.

For the full year, Equinox (EQX) guided for Greenstone production of 110K-130K oz, well below its prior guidance of 175K-205K oz.

data.title: Robotaxi pure-play: Pony AI plans for an IPO on the Nasdaq
data.description:
Chinese autonomous vehicle upstart Pony AI filed for an IPO on the Nasdaq under the trading symbol PONY. The company did not indicate the size of its public offering of shares.

Pony AI, founded in December 2016 by James Peng and Tiancheng Lou, is a global autonomous vehicle technology company with operations in Silicon Valley, Beijing, and Guangzhou. The co-founders, both former software developers for Baidu, established Pony.ai with the goal of advancing autonomous driving technology. Since its inception, Pony.ai has made significant strides in the industry. In April 2019, the company launched a pilot system in Guangzhou, covering 50 km² for employees and invited affiliates. Later that year, they began a trial in Irvine, California. The company has secured partnerships with major manufacturers like Toyota (TM) and obtained permits for driverless ride-hailing services in Chinese cities. In 2024, Pony AI teamed up with Alibaba's (BABA) online mapping unit Amap in an accelerated push to roll out robotaxis in China.

In its SEC filing, Pony AI said it currently operates a fleet of over 250 robotaxis, which has accumulated over 33.5 million kilometers of autonomous driving mileage, including over 3.9 million kilometers of driverless mileage. The company also highlighted that it was among the first in China to obtain licenses to operate fully driverless robotaxis in all four Tier-1 cities in China (namely Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou and Shenzhen), and is the only autonomous driving technology company that has obtained all available regulatory permits essential for providing public-facing robotaxi services within these Tier-1 cities. Pony AI generated revenue of $24.7 million for the six-month period that ended on June 30. A net loss of $51.8 million was recorded for those six months.

The race for robotaxi development and deployment includes, to varying degrees, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), Waymo (GOOG), Amazon's (AMZN) Zoox, May Mobility, Baidu's (BIDU) Apollo, General Motors' (GM) Cruise, WeRide, AutoX, Aurora Innovation (AUR), and Motional (APTV) (OTCPK:HYMTF). Within the group of robotaxi fleet aspirants, Tesla (TSLA) has been the most vocal about creating a fleet that allows drivers to rent out their personal vehicles to the fleet.

data.title: United Microelectronics downgraded at Goldman Sachs
data.description:
• United Microelectronics (NYSE:UMC) was downgraded at Goldman Sachs on Thursday as the research firm believes pricing pressure will last longer at foundries catering towards mature nodes.
  • "Following our estimate cut on pricing for 28nm and above mature nodes of [Taiwan Semiconductor] in 2025E, we are lowering our UMC ... earnings forecast in response to the more intensified pricing competition for the legacy foundry industry into 20225E and beyond, mainly on the back of: further [average selling price] erosion due to ongoing competition especially from mainland China on capacity build; and a still subdued demand outlook for consumer electronics, PC, smartphones, auto, industrial, etc., ...," analyst Bruce Lu wrote in a note to clients.
  • Lu cut his rating on United Microelectronics to Neutral and the price target was cut to $7.40.
  • United Microelectronics shares rose 1.4% in afternoon trading on Thursday.

data.title: William Blair upgrades Baldwin Group with 'growing pains' in past
data.description:
• William Blair upgraded Baldwin Insurance Group (NASDAQ:BWIN) from market perform to outperform, where Baldwin has pushed past its growing pains.
  • Shares are trading up 4.83% in midday session.
  • Given strong double-digit organic growth potential, enabled in part by a differentiated personal lines business, the prospect for more consistent margin improvement and adjusted EPS growth, and a step-up in free cash flow that should support reinvestment and tuck-in M&A activity.
  • WBLR believes Baldwin will be increasingly viewed as a quality compounder that can grow EPS in the 20% to 30% range over the medium to long term.
  • WBLR's conservative base-case estimate for 2025 adjusted EPS is $2.03 vs. consensus of $2.07.
  • Also, assuming a valuation of 33 times adjusted P/E implies roughly 25% upside.
  • Looking at Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, BWIN has a score of 3.34 out of 5. The company received B in the prospect of profitability, while it got an A+ in momentum and growth factors.
  • Turning to the Wall Street community stands with a Buy, almost 4 analysts gave BWIN a Strong Buy while 2 stood with Buy whereas, 2 of them gave the stock a Hold recommendation, while none were on the short side.

data.title: Google again set to block election ads after polls close: report
data.description:
Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) is again apparently set to block election ads across all its platforms with the closing of the last polls on Election Day, a continuing move to prevent voting misinformation.

That's a policy it introduced with the 2020 election, and the tech giant is set to block the ads at poll closing again in 2024, according to a memo sent to ad partners and reported by Axios.

The company will pause all U.S. ads related to U.S. elections as of the closing of the last polls on Nov. 5, Axios said, citing the email.

That comes from an "abundance of caution and to limit the potential for confusion, given the likelihood that votes will continue to be counted after Election Day," Axios hears from a Google spokesperson.

Google had pointed to its work preparing for the U.S. elections in a blog post at the end of August.

In 2020, Google lifted that post-election ban in December -- well after Election Day but ahead of a runoff election in Georgia. That's expected to be the case again this election.

Social rival Meta Platforms (META) will block new political, electoral and social issue ads during the final week of the campaign, the report points out.

data.title: Inflation wave is retreating, government debt wave is not, IMF's Georgieva says
data.description:
The good news is that "the big global inflation wave is in retreat," International Monetary Fund Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva said in a speech on Thursday, previewing the fund's global economic outlook to be released next week.

"A combination of resolute monetary policy action, easing supply chain constraints, and moderating food and energy prices is guiding us back in the direction of price stability," she said.

That has been achieved "without tipping the global economy into recession and large-scale job losses," she noted. Specifically, U.S. and euro area labor markets are "cooling in an orderly manner."

But Georgieva cautions that there are a number of forces that can derail growth and counteract disinflation.

A difficult geopolitical environment, particularly the expanding conflict in the Middle East, holds the potential to destabilize regional economies and global oil and gas markets.

Also, the combination of low growth and high debt puts economies at risk, especially in emerging markets. Medium-term forecasts expect growth to be lower than before the pandemic, though not sharply lower. That means, it's "not enough to eradicate world poverty," Georgieva said. "Nor to create the number of jobs we require. Nor to generate the tax revenues that governments need to service heavy debt loans while attending to vast investment needs, including the green transition."

As the funds needed to service government debt grow, the amount of capital available to operate and invest becomes smaller. "As debt increases, fiscal space contracts disproportionately more in low-income countries—not all debt burdens are made the same," she said.

She says there's plenty that can be done to address the risks facing the global economy. Reducing debt and rebuilding buffers' and reforming job markets to enhance skill sets and enhance productivity are two such areas.

Countries can also work together to preserve rules-based global trading, mitigate the effects of climate change, and utilizing AI to boost productivity, Georgieva said. In other words, her answer to protect the economic progress made is global cooperation.
